vocabulary.definition: A bar graph that shows the frequency of data within equal intervals; bars touch with no gaps
vocabulary.visual: Bars side by side: 0-9 pts (3 players), 10-19 pts (8 players), 20-29 pts (4 players)

vocabulary.definition: The number of times a data value or group of values occurs
vocabulary.visual: If 5 players scored 10-19 points, the frequency for that interval is 5

vocabulary.definition: A range of values used to group data in a histogram
vocabulary.visual: 0-9, 10-19, 20-29 are intervals of width 10 — each covers 10 values

vocabulary.definition: The way data values are spread out or arranged across possible values
vocabulary.visual: Most data in the middle with fewer at the ends = bell-shaped; most on one side with a tail = skewed

vocabulary.definition: The shape and spread of a data set
vocabulary.visual: A histogram that is tallest in the middle and shorter on both sides is symmetric

vocabulary.definition: How spread out the values in a data set are
vocabulary.visual: Data in just 2 intervals = low variability. Data across 6 intervals = high variability

launch.narrative: The basketball league has 30 players and wants to display everyone's points-per-game average in a way that shows how the data is distributed. The raw averages are: 2, 5, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 11, 12, 13, 13, 14, 14, 15, 15, 16, 16, 17, 18, 18, 19, 20, 21, 22, 23, 24, 25, 28, 30, 32. Listing all 30 numbers is hard to read — a histogram will reveal the pattern!
launch.contextImage: League scoreboard with 30 player names and scoring averages
noticePrompts: Where do most of the scoring averages seem to cluster?
Are there many players at the very low or very high end?
How could you group these numbers to see a pattern?
wonderPrompts: What size intervals would work best for this data?
What shape will the data make when grouped into a histogram?

explore.label: Create a frequency table for the league scoring data using intervals of 10. Then describe the histogram shape.

discourse.prompt: The tallest bar is the 10–19 interval with 15 players. What does the shape of this histogram tell you about scoring in the league?
discourse.sentenceFrame: Most players score between ___ and ___ points per game. The histogram is skewed ___ because ___. This tells me that ___.

optional.stem: A histogram differs from a bar graph because...
choices: It groups numeric data into equal intervals with no gaps
optional.explanation: Histograms display continuous numeric data in intervals (bins) with bars touching.

optional.stem: A histogram bin for 10–19 has a bar height of 6. What does 6 represent?
choices: 6 data values fall in that interval
optional.explanation: The bar height is the frequency — how many values fall in that bin.

optional.stem: Histogram bins are 0–9, 10–19, 20–29 with heights 4, 9, 2. How many data values total?
Why should histogram intervals be equal in width?
choices: So the bars fairly compare how many values fall in each range
optional.explanation: Equal widths make frequency comparisons fair across bins.

approaching.stem: A histogram shows these frequencies: 0–4: 3 players, 5–9: 8 players, 10–14: 12 players, 15–19: 5 players. How many players are represented in total?
In a histogram, what does the height of each bar represent?
choices: The frequency (count) of data in that interval
The average of the data in that interval
approaching.explanation: Each bar's height shows the frequency — how many data values fall within that interval.

approaching.stem: How is a histogram different from a regular bar graph?
choices: Histogram bars touch (no gaps) because the intervals are continuous ranges
Histograms use colors and bar graphs don't
Bar graphs show numbers and histograms show words
approaching.explanation: In a histogram, bars touch because each interval connects to the next (0–9, 10–19, etc.) — the data is continuous.

approaching.stem: A histogram has intervals: 0–4, 5–9, 10–14, 15–19. What is the width of each interval?
approaching.explanation: Each interval covers 5 values (0,1,2,3,4 then 5,6,7,8,9 etc.), so the width is 5.

approaching.instructions: Put the steps for creating a histogram in the correct order.

categories.label: Steps (in order)
items: Step 1: Choose equal-sized intervals that cover all the data
Step 2: Count how many data values fall in each interval (frequency)
Step 3: Draw bars for each interval — height = frequency
Step 4: Make sure bars touch with no gaps

hints: You must decide on intervals before counting data in each one.
Histogram bars always touch because the data ranges are continuous.
approaching.label: Order the steps for making a histogram.

onLevel.stem: A histogram of test scores shows: 50–59: 2, 60–69: 5, 70–79: 10, 80–89: 8, 90–99: 3. Which interval has the most students?
onLevel.explanation: The 70–79 interval has the highest frequency of 10 students.

pairs.match: Bars touch, shows frequency by interval
Count of values in an interval

onLevel.label: Match each term to its description.

onLevel.instructions: Based on the histogram frequencies below, sort the intervals from most players to fewest: 0–9: 4, 10–19: 11, 20–29: 7, 30–39: 2.
hints: The tallest bar (most players) goes first.
onLevel.label: Rank the intervals from highest to lowest frequency.

extending.title: Find the Histogram Error

workedExample.label: Conclusion
workedExample.work: The histogram is symmetric because the bars go 1, 2, 3, 4, 2.

extending.correctWork: The frequencies are correct (1, 2, 3, 4, 2), but this is NOT symmetric. In a symmetric histogram, the bars rise and fall evenly from the center. Here the bars increase from left to right and only drop at the end — the data is skewed LEFT (most scores are on the higher end).
hints: Symmetric means the left side mirrors the right side. Does 1, 2, 3, 4, 2 mirror evenly?
The peak (4) is in the 80–89 range, which is toward the right. That means most data is on the high end.

extending.prompt: A teacher collected test scores and made two different histograms — one with intervals of 5 (50–54, 55–59, etc.) and one with intervals of 20 (50–69, 70–89, 90–109). Both use the same data. How might the two histograms look different? Which interval size gives you more detail about the distribution? When might the larger interval be better?
extending.sentenceFrame: The histogram with intervals of 5 would have ___ bars and show ___. The histogram with intervals of 20 would have ___ bars and show ___. Smaller intervals are better when ___, and larger intervals are better when ___.

rows.bars: Bars rise evenly to a peak in the center, then fall evenly
rows.example: Player heights on a team — most are average height, fewer are very tall or very short

rows.bars: Tallest bars on the left, with a tail stretching to the right
rows.example: Points scored — most players score low, a few stars score very high

rows.bars: Tallest bars on the right, with a tail stretching to the left
rows.example: Free-throw percentages of experienced players — most are high, a few are very low

extending.label: Match each histogram shape with its description and a sports example.

connect.scenario: A soccer coach looks at a histogram of shots-per-game for the team: 0–2 shots: 1 player, 3–5 shots: 8 players, 6–8 shots: 10 players, 9–11 shots: 3 players, 12–14 shots: 1 player. The coach says, 'Most of my players take a good number of shots.'
connect.promptQuestion: Does the histogram support the coach's claim? What does the shape of the data tell you?
connect.prompt: The histogram peaks at the ___ interval with ___ players. The shape is approximately ___. This ___ the coach's claim because ___.

exitTicket.stem: A histogram of player heights shows: 60–63 in: 2, 64–67 in: 7, 68–71 in: 9, 72–75 in: 4. Which interval has the most players?
exitTicket.explanation: The 68–71 inch interval has the highest frequency of 9 players.
